title: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions

items.subtopic: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MECHANISMS UNDERLYING DYSPNEA
items.title: Dyspnea Prevalence and Predictive Value
items.content: Dyspnea is a common symptom with significant prevalence, affecting up to 50% of inpatients and 25% of ambulatory patients. It increases to 37% in adults aged ≥70 years. Dyspnea is a major cause of emergency room visits, accounting for 3–4 million annually. In chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), dyspnea severity may better predict outcomes than forced expiratory volume in 1 second ($FEV_1$).
keyPoints: Prevalence: up to one-half of inpatients, one-quarter of ambulatory patients.
Community prevalence: 9–13%, increasing to 37% for adults ≥70 years.
Emergency room visits: 3–4 million per year due to dyspnea.
Predictive value: Dyspnea may better predict outcomes than $FEV_1$ in COPD.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MECHANISMS UNDERLYING DYSPNEA

items.subtopic: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MECHANISMS UNDERLYING DYSPNEA
items.title: Mechanisms of Dyspnea: Afferent Pathways
items.content: Dyspnea arises from complex mechanisms involving afferent signals to the CNS. These include activation of peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid body, aortic arch) and central chemoreceptors (medulla) by hypoxemia, hypercapnia, or acidemia, leading to 'air hunger.' Mechanoreceptors in airways, lungs, and chest wall respond to increased workload from diseases like asthma or COPD, causing symptoms such as chest tightness.
keyPoints: Afferent signals: Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid body, aortic arch), central chemoreceptors (medulla).
Triggers: Hypoxemia, hypercapnia, acidemia leading to 'air hunger.'
Mechanoreceptors: Upper airways, lungs, chest wall responding to increased workload.
Symptoms: Chest tightness in asthma or COPD.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MECHANISMS UNDERLYING DYSPNEA

items.subtopic: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MECHANISMS UNDERLYING DYSPNEA
items.title: Efferent Pathways and Psychological Influence on Dyspnea
items.content: Dyspnea involves efferent signals from the CNS to respiratory muscles, contributing to sensations of respiratory effort or 'work of breathing.' Corollary discharge to the sensory cortex may enhance sensations like 'air hunger,' especially under increased ventilatory load in diseases such as COPD. Psychological factors like fear or anxiety can exacerbate dyspnea by intensifying physiological disturbances.
keyPoints: Efferent signals: CNS (motor cortex, brainstem) to respiratory muscles.
Sensations: Respiratory effort ('work of breathing'), 'air hunger.'
Psychological influence: Fear or anxiety heightening dyspnea sensation.
Disease context: Increased ventilatory load in COPD.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MECHANISMS UNDERLYING DYSPNEA

items.title: Importance of Formal Dyspnea Assessment in COPD Management
items.content: In managing Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), it is crucial to formally assess dyspnea using validated tools such as the Modified Medical Research Council Dyspnea Scale. This assessment helps quantify symptom/impact burden, which is essential for tailoring patient management strategies.
keyPoints: Dyspnea should be assessed in a context relevant and beneficial for patient management.
The GOLD criteria recommend using the Modified Medical Research Council Dyspnea Scale for COPD patients.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · ASSESSING DYSPNEA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Approach to Chronic Dyspnea: Identifying Underlying Causes
items.content: Chronic dyspnea, defined as symptoms lasting longer than 1 month, often stems from pulmonary or cardiac conditions. A systematic approach starting with history and physical examination, followed by targeted laboratory tests and diagnostics, is essential for identifying the underlying cause.
keyPoints: Chronic dyspnea lasts more than 1 month and can be due to pulmonary or cardiac causes in up to 85% of cases.
A graded diagnostic approach begins with history/physical examination, followed by laboratory tests and additional diagnostics if needed.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · ASSESSING DYSPNEA | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Approach to Dyspnea Evaluation: Known vs. Unknown Etiologies
items.content: In patients with known pulmonary, cardiac, or neuromuscular conditions presenting with worsening dyspnea, the evaluation should focus on whether there is progression of the existing condition or a new process causing symptoms. For those without prior conditions, identifying an underlying etiology is crucial as treatment varies significantly based on the cause. Initial history and physical examination are fundamental, followed by diagnostic testing that may lead to subspecialty referral if needed.
keyPoints: Two-thirds of patients require diagnostic tests beyond initial clinical presentation.
Initial evaluation focuses on progression or new process in known conditions; etiology identification in unknown cases.
History and physical examination are fundamental, followed by indicated diagnostic testing.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · OVERALL | HISTORY

items.title: Characterizing Dyspnea: Symptom Descriptors and Associated Conditions
items.content: Patient descriptions of dyspnea can guide etiology determination. Chest tightness may indicate bronchoconstriction, while inability to take a deep breath suggests dynamic hyperinflation from COPD. Orthopnea is linked with CHF, diaphragm impairment due to obesity, or asthma triggered by esophageal reflux. Nocturnal dyspnea points to CHF or asthma. Acute episodes suggest myocardial ischemia, bronchospasm, or pulmonary embolism; chronic symptoms are typical of COPD, interstitial lung disease, and chronic thromboembolic disease.
keyPoints: Chest tightness suggests bronchoconstriction.
Inability to take a deep breath correlates with dynamic hyperinflation from COPD.
Orthopnea indicates CHF, diaphragm impairment, or asthma triggered by reflux.
Nocturnal dyspnea suggests CHF or asthma.
Acute episodes may reflect myocardial ischemia, bronchospasm, or pulmonary embolism.
Chronic persistent dyspnea is typical of COPD, interstitial lung disease, and chronic thromboembolic disease.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · OVERALL | HISTORY

items.title: Significance of Vital Signs in Respiratory and Circulatory Disorders
items.content: Vital signs provide crucial initial clues to underlying etiologies in respiratory and circulatory disorders. Specific patterns such as fever, hypertension, tachycardia, and hypoxemia can indicate distinct pathophysiological processes.
keyPoints: Fever may suggest an infectious or inflammatory process.
Hypertension in heart failure could point to diastolic dysfunction.
Tachycardia might be related to fever, cardiac dysfunction, or deconditioning.
Resting hypoxemia suggests hypercapnia, ventilation-perfusion mismatch, shunt, or impaired diffusion capacity.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Assessment of Respiratory and Cardiac Findings in Physical Examination
items.content: Physical examination should include assessment of respiratory rate, pulsus paradoxus, chest symmetry, percussion, auscultation, and cardiac signs. These assessments help identify specific conditions such as COPD, asthma, pleural effusion, pneumothorax, and heart failure.
keyPoints: Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg on inspiration suggests COPD, acute asthma, or pericardial disease.
Chest percussion: Dullness indicates pleural effusion; hyperresonance suggests pneumothorax or emphysema.
Auscultation findings: Wheezes and rhonchi indicate airway disorders; rales suggest interstitial edema or fibrosis.
Cardiac signs: Jugular venous distention, S3/S4 gallops, and murmurs help identify elevated right heart pressures, left ventricular dysfunction, and valvular disease.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: When to Order Chest Imaging for Respiratory and Circulatory Assessment
items.content: Chest radiographs are indicated after history and physical examination if the diagnosis remains unclear. They help assess lung volumes, identify interstitial disease, infiltrates, emphysema, pulmonary vasculature changes, cardiac silhouette enlargement, and pleural effusions. CT is reserved for further evaluation of interstitial lung disease or suspected pulmonary embolism.
keyPoints: Chest radiograph should be obtained if diagnosis remains elusive after history and physical examination.
Hyperinflation suggests obstructive lung disease; low lung volumes indicate interstitial edema, fibrosis, diaphragmatic dysfunction, or impaired chest wall motion.
Prominent pulmonary vasculature in upper zones indicates pulmonary venous hypertension; enlarged central pulmonary arteries suggest pulmonary arterial hypertension.
Enlarged cardiac silhouette can point toward dilated cardiomyopathy or valvular disease.
Bilateral pleural effusions are typical of CHF and some collagen-vascular diseases; unilateral effusions raise suspicion for carcinoma, pulmonary embolism, heart failure, or parapneumonic effusion.
CT is reserved for further evaluation of interstitial lung disease or suspected pulmonary embolism.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · CHEST IMAGING | LABORATORY STUDIES

items.title: Laboratory Studies in Evaluating Dyspnea and Cardiopulmonary Conditions
items.content: Initial laboratory tests for dyspnea include hematocrit to exclude anemia, basic metabolic panel to assess acidosis or bicarbonate levels indicating CO2 retention. ECG is used to detect ventricular hypertrophy or myocardial infarction. Spirometry identifies obstructive or restrictive ventilatory defects. Echocardiography is indicated for suspected systolic dysfunction, pulmonary hypertension, or valvular disease. BNP measurement helps assess CHF but may be elevated with right ventricular strain.
keyPoints: Hematocrit should be checked to exclude occult anemia as a cause of reduced oxygen-carrying capacity.
Basic metabolic panel can help identify significant underlying metabolic acidosis; elevated bicarbonate might indicate CO2 retention in chronic respiratory failure.
Electrocardiography is used to detect ventricular hypertrophy and prior myocardial infarction.
Spirometry can diagnose obstructive ventilatory defects and suggest restrictive defects, prompting further pulmonary function testing.
Echocardiography is indicated for suspected systolic dysfunction, pulmonary hypertension, or valvular heart disease.
Brain natriuretic peptide levels are used to assess CHF but may be elevated with right ventricular strain.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · CHEST IMAGING | LABORATORY STUDIES

items.subtopic: DISTINGUISHING CARDIOVASCULAR FROM RESPIRATORY SYSTEM DYSPNEA | Dyspnea
items.title: Utilizing CPET to Differentiate Between Cardiovascular and Respiratory Causes of Dyspnea
items.content: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) is a valuable tool for distinguishing whether dyspnea is primarily due to cardiovascular or respiratory system abnormalities. It involves incremental symptom-limited exercises with measurements of ventilation, pulmonary gas exchange, and sometimes includes noninvasive and invasive measures of pulmonary vascular pressures and cardiac output.
keyPoints: If at peak exercise the patient achieves predicted maximal ventilation, shows an increase in dead space or hypoxemia, or develops bronchospasm, the respiratory system is likely responsible.

Cardiovascular causes are suggested if heart rate exceeds 85% of predicted maximum, anaerobic threshold occurs early, blood pressure becomes excessively high or decreases during exercise, $O_{2}$ pulse falls, or ischemic changes appear on ECG.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · DISTINGUISHING CARDIOVASCULAR FROM RESPIRATORY SYSTEM DYSPNEA | Dyspnea

items.subtopic: DISTINGUISHING CARDIOVASCULAR FROM RESPIRATORY SYSTEM DYSPNEA | Dyspnea
items.title: Management Strategies for Persistent Dyspnea
items.content: The management of dyspnea involves correcting underlying conditions and addressing reversible causes. For persistent cases, termed 'chronic breathlessness syndrome,' strategies focus on symptom relief and improving quality of life.
keyPoints: Supplemental O₂ is recommended if resting O₂ saturation ≤88% or drops to these levels with activity or sleep.
Pulmonary rehabilitation programs improve dyspnea, exercise capacity, and reduce hospitalizations in COPD patients.
Opioids may reduce air hunger but should be used cautiously due to risks of respiratory depression; recent studies question their benefit in COPD.
Anxiolytics have not shown consistent benefits for dyspnea.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · DISTINGUISHING CARDIOVASCULAR FROM RESPIRATORY SYSTEM DYSPNEA | Dyspnea

items.question: What is the term used for the mismatch in integrative signaling between afferent and efferent pathways?
items.explanation: The passage describes the mismatch in integrative signaling between afferent and efferent pathways as 'efferent-referent mismatch'.

items.question: Which conditions are most commonly responsible for chronic dyspnea?
items.explanation: Pulmonary or cardiac conditions are most commonly responsible for chronic dyspnea.

items.question: In patients without a prior known potential cause of dyspnea, what is the initial focus of evaluation?
options: Determining if the condition has progressed.
Focusing on determining an underlying etiology.
Referring immediately to a subspecialty clinic.
items.explanation: The initial evaluation focuses on determining an underlying etiology for patients without a known potential cause of dyspnea.

items.question: Which symptom is commonly associated with congestive heart failure (CHF), mechanical impairment of the diaphragm, or asthma triggered by esophageal reflux?
items.explanation: Orthopnea is a common indicator of congestive heart failure, mechanical impairment of the diaphragm associated with obesity, or asthma triggered by esophageal reflux.

items.question: What condition should be considered when a patient experiences dyspnea in an upright position that is relieved by lying supine?
options: Left atrial myxoma or hepatopulmonary syndrome.
items.explanation: Platypnea, which is dyspnea in the upright position with relief in the supine position, should prompt consideration of left atrial myxoma or hepatopulmonary syndrome.
items.reference: Section 5 Alterations in Circulatory and Respiratory Functions · OVERALL | HISTORY

items.question: Which physical finding suggests the presence of diaphragmatic weakness?

Rounding of the abdomen during exhalation
Use of accessory muscles of ventilation
items.explanation: Inward motion of the abdomen during inspiration is a sign of diaphragmatic weakness.

items.question: What does pulsus paradoxus greater than 10 mmHg on inspiration suggest?

items.explanation: A decrease in systolic pressure by >10 mmHg on inspiration suggests COPD, acute asthma, or pericardial disease.

items.question: Which physical sign is indicative of pulmonary edema?

items.question: Which chest radiograph finding is most consistent with pulmonary venous hypertension?

items.explanation: The assertion is false because dyspnea can arise from various causes including cardiac and neurologic conditions, not just pulmonary.

items.assertion: Acute, intermittent episodes of dyspnea are more likely to reflect episodes of myocardial ischemia.
items.reason: Myocardial ischemia can cause acute and episodic symptoms due to transient reductions in blood flow to the heart muscle.
